cataloging_subject.label: The Laud Troy Book
cataloging_subject.scope_note: Count the complete poem once. The single BODY book division and 667 LG elements are representation structures, not 668 intellectual works and not a proved literary stanza count.

agents.name: J. Ernst Wülfing
notes: Editor of the 1902-1903 EETS source edition; not the author of the medieval work.

assertion.notes: No medieval author is named in the encoded work or Wülfing's source study. Catalog the poet as anonymous rather than assigning the editor, source-work authors, or holding library; DMER is not used as evidence for this responsibility.

scope.description: The 1902-1903 EETS source edition of all 18,664 numbered lines.

notes: Retained to make the catalog-summary source-edition editor display resolvable; edition details also belong to lineage.

assertion.notes: Retain the verbal source attribution without inventing numeric bounds. Do not substitute the manuscript catalog's 1400-1410 production date for the work-composition date.

notes: Current DMER localization includes a question mark.

places.label: North-West Midlands
notes: Historical Wülfing hypothesis; terminology normalized for display.

assertion.notes: Preserve as a source-attributed historical hypothesis rather than reconciling it silently with current DMER.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Latin rubrics and the Latin/French source works do not change the primary language of the Middle English work.

form_statements.description: A complete 18,664-line Middle English poem in rhyming four-stress couplets, described by DMER as aa4 bb4 and so on.

subject_statements.label: Military combat, siege, treachery, and chivalric action
Conversion, religious figures, sacrament, marriage, sexual encounters, monsters, dreams, and the supernatural

assertion.notes: This grouped display projects DMER's current controlled theme set without reviving an older keyword list.

summaries.value: An anonymous early-fifteenth-century Middle English Troy romance in 18,664 rhyming four-stress couplet lines, translating and adapting Guido delle Colonne's Latin Historia destructionis Troiae and possibly drawing directly on Benoît de Sainte-Maure's Roman de Troie. It narrates the origins, siege, and destruction of Troy and survives uniquely in Bodleian Library MS. Laud Misc. 595.

The record covers only the complete medieval English poem. Wülfing's two publication parts, edition apparatus, print carriers, page facsimiles, catalog records, XML/TXT encodings, and archive package are lineage layers rather than additional work parts.

extent.description: One complete verse work represented by one BODY DIV1 book, 667 encoded LG groups, and 18,664 L elements. The 667 groups are encoding/navigation units, many lacking a stanza type, and are not asserted as 667 literary stanzas or separate content items.

content_parts.label: The Laud Troy Book text
content_parts.summary: The complete single Middle English Troy romance, lines 1-18,664.

locators.value: TEXT/BODY/DIV1; generated NODE APE9595.0001.001:2; L elements 1-18,664
ff. 1r-275v; numbered verse ends 275r and terminal rubric is reported on 275v

notes: Wülfing's publication split after line 10,876 is an edition/publication boundary, not a division of the medieval work into two works.
The edition's 667 LG groups are retained as representation metrics only.

related_works.title: Historia destructionis Troiae
notes: Confirmed direct Latin source at high confidence; no surviving physical exemplar used by the English poet is identified.

related_works.title: Roman de Troie
notes: Possible direct source at medium confidence, but similarities may instead be mediated through Guido; do not upgrade this to confirmed adaptation_of without stronger evidence.

open_questions.question: How should current dialect scholarship reconcile DMER's East Midlands? localization with Wülfing's older North-West Midlands hypothesis?
next_steps: Preserve both as source-attributed tentative statements; do not resolve by preference.

open_questions.question: Did the Laud poet consult Benoît's Roman de Troie directly, or are the parallels mediated through Guido?
next_steps: Keep the metadata relation related_to and the lineage relation tentative at medium confidence, grounded in Wülfing and ARLIMA rather than DMER.

notes: Describe the medieval poem, not Wülfing's two-volume publication or the U-M digital item.
Count one work once and use one count-bearing content part.
Use sections=1 for the single BODY work division and verse_groups=667 for the encoding groups; do not call all 667 groups literary stanzas.
Keep the witness's 1400-1410 production date separate from the work's early-fifteenth-century composition date.
Keep the work's tentative dialect region separate from the manuscript's broad English origin.
Keep source-work authors Guido and Benoît out of the target-work author responsibility.
Do not normalize the early-fifteenth-century composition statement to 1400-1425; no inspected source supplies those exact bounds.
The poem is not authored by Wülfing, Guido delle Colonne, Benoît de Sainte-Maure, or the Bodleian Library.
The 667 LG elements are not proved to be 667 authorial stanzas.
The publication split at lines 10,876/10,877 is not a two-work boundary.
The manuscript's 1400-1410 date is not automatically the work's exact composition date.
A surviving physical Latin or French exemplar used by the English poet has not been identified.
Direct use of Benoît is not confirmed.
